coredata.dc:title: Facile reduction of graphene using urea in solid phase and surface modification by N-doped graphene quantum dots for adsorption of organic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Khojasteh, Hossein
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Safajou, Hamed
Safardoust-Hojaghan, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: Generally, hydrazine reducing method is a common way to prepare the solution-processable graphene from graphite oxide. High toxicity of the hydrazine is the main problem which limits it's using in large-scale implementation. So a green approach for the preparation of graphene from graphite oxide is needed. Here, we introduced a very simple and scalable procedure for efficient reduction of graphite oxide in solid phase, mild temperature reaction and ambient atmosphere. Urea was used as expansion–reduction agent and well mixed with graphite oxide. The mixture was heated at 160°C for 48h. During the reaction, urea decomposes upon heating and releases reducing gases release to remove oxygen containing groups of graphite oxide. After removing residual the unreacted urea by washing with water, the product was dispersed in ethylene glycol and exposed to ultrasound irradiation to obtain well dispersed and de‑oxygenated graphene or reduced graphene oxide (RGO). The obtained results suggest replacing of hydrazine in the reduction of graphite oxide suspensions by a nontoxic and safe reductant agent to produce RGO with high quality and efficiency. In the next step, the obtained RGO was modified by using N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-RGO) to produce an absorbent with high surface area and increased amount of surface functional groups. Experiments indicate that final product has interesting capability to remove organic pollutants from polluted water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reduced graphene oxide
Nanostructures
N-RGO
Urea, adsorbent

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Expansion and reduction of GO to RGO
Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Synthesis of RGO modified with N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-RGO)
Morphology of GO, graphene sheets and N-GQDs
Effect of surface modification on the adsorption efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The XRD pattern of graphite, GO and RGO, b) Magnified XRD pattern around the (002) peak.
Magnified XRD pattern around the (001) peak.
FTIR spectrum of graphite oxide and RGO.
SEM images of (a) natural graphite, (b) GO and (c) synthesized RGO.
Raman spectra obtained using 785nm laser excitation.
TEM images of urea assisted synthesis of RGO at lower and higher magnifications.
Thermogravimetric analysis: a) graphite powders, b) GO and c) the as-prepared RGO.
FT-IR spectrum of RGO modified by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-RGO).

Calibration of the Kb values of a series of dyes' solutions. a) RhB, b) MB, c) MO. For each dye, the Kb is calibrated through measuring the absorbance of the dye's solutions with a series of normal concentrations.
The comparison of adsorption capacity of RGO and N-RGO products at similar adsorption condition for different dyes (t=720min, pH=7, W=0.05g, C0 =40ppm, V=50ml).
Nitrogen Adsorption-Desorption isotherm of a) N- GQDs sample and b) RGO/N- GQDs composite.
Schematic illustration of the RGO synthesis procedure.
Representative list of plants and extract of parts used for green reduction of graphite oxide (GO).
The adsorption capacities of the N-RGO for different dyes.

head.ce:title: Facile reduction of graphene using urea in solid phase and surface modification by N-doped graphene quantum dots for adsorption of organic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box, 87317–51167, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Generally, hydrazine reducing method is a common way to prepare the solution-processable graphene from graphite oxide. High toxicity of the hydrazine is the main problem which limits it's using in large-scale implementation. So a green approach for the preparation of graphene from graphite oxide is needed. Here, we introduced a very simple and scalable procedure for efficient reduction of graphite oxide in solid phase, mild temperature reaction and ambient atmosphere. Urea was used as expansion–reduction agent and well mixed with graphite oxide. The mixture was heated at 160°C for 48h. During the reaction, urea decomposes upon heating and releases reducing gases release to remove oxygen containing groups of graphite oxide. After removing residual the unreacted urea by washing with water, the product was dispersed in ethylene glycol and exposed to ultrasound irradiation to obtain well dispersed and de‑oxygenated graphene or reduced graphene oxide (RGO). The obtained results suggest replacing of hydrazine in the reduction of graphite oxide suspensions by a nontoxic and safe reductant agent to produce RGO with high quality and efficiency. In the next step, the obtained RGO was modified by using N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-RGO) to produce an absorbent with high surface area and increased amount of surface functional groups. Experiments indicate that final product has interesting capability to remove organic pollutants from polluted water.

ce:section.ce:para: So far graphene is the world's thinnest, strongest, and stiffest material with lightweight, as well as being an excellent conductor of heat and electricity. When graphite is oxidized to separate the carbon layers with oxygen molecules, an oxidized form of graphene which is called graphene oxide (GO) will be provided [12]. Graphene oxide is an appropriate source for converting to reduce graphene oxide (RGO) [13,14]. GO is desirable due to its low cost, easy access, scalability, non-toxic and widespread ability [15]. In order to benefit graphene aspects, large scale production of graphene is required. Several procedures like chemical vapor deposition (CVD), direct exfoliation of graphene in organic solvents, epitaxial growth on electrically insulating surfaces, microwave-assisted exfoliation, electric arc discharge, thermal exfoliation of graphite oxide, electrolytic exfoliation and solution-based chemical reduction of GO have been reported previously for the preparation of graphene [16–22].

The primary and usual method for GO production is conventional modified Hummers method [23,24]. There are a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, carbonyl and epoxy groups on both sides of a single GO sheet, so these oxidized materials can well disperse in aqueous environments which such dispersions are very facilitative for further processing and characterization. These implanted functional groups overcome the van der Waals forces between graphite sheets and enlarges the interlayer spacing. However, reactions on the surface of graphene sheet disturb its planar structure followed by destruction of the sp2 structure (the π-orbital structure). This event leads to the formation of defects and loss of electrical and thermal conductivity [25]. So for an effective conversion of GO into graphene some modifications need to be applied.

Various reduction methods such as thermal annealing, electrochemical, or chemical approaches have been introduced for reduction of graphene oxide [26–29]. Among them, the wet chemistry reduction techniques which employ graphite oxide as precursor and reducing agents such as hydrazine, hydroquinone or sodium borohydride for elimination of oxygen groups, is the most promising and practical method. This procedure is the most promising and practical method and is extensively applied for the large-scale production of highly reduced graphene oxide [30–32]. But these chemical reducing agents have some drawbacks like the toxic nature, hazardous, and harmful to both environment and human life, therefore their use should be avoided in the large-scale implementation of this approach [33].

In the investigations of replacing an eco-friendly and safety reducing agent instead of toxic agents, two features should be taken into account. First, the new reducing agent should be as effective as previous ones in order to eliminate the oxygen groups on GO. Second, for the sake of further processability, the reduced material should produce sheets with stable suspensions without needing to surfactants or any other stabilizers [34]. Consequently, new approaches need to be explored for the effective conversion of GO into RGO sheets under mild and moderate conditions by utilizing non-toxic and environmentally friendly reducing agents. Previous studies indicates that, some green approaches to the reduction of graphene oxide, consists of using different parts of plants like leaves, peel, root etc., that have the potential to act as reducing agents in the bio-reduction and formation of functional graphene from GO. These procedures mostly contain green materials such as alkaloids, pectin's, proteins, vitamins, amino acids, saccharides, alcoholic compounds, flavonoids and enzymes (Table 1 ) [35]. But the main problem of these reductants, is that all of them are in aqueous phase that makes some difficult about its separation and purification. Also their efficiency in reduction is not satisfying and desirable.

In the present search, we introduce a novel approach to produce reduced graphene oxide (RGO) from graphite oxide in in a mild reaction condition. Mostly common preparation methods of grapheme contain colloid approaches to reduce graphite oxide. But here we have used moderate temperature, environmental atmosphere and urea as a cheap, facile single-source molecular precursor and a solid expansion–reducing agent for preparation of graphene.

As we know, urea is a common and low-cost raw material in the chemical industry. Urea is a colorless, odorless solid, highly soluble in water, neither acidic nor alkaline and most importantly, practically non-toxic material [36]. When urea is heated to temperature around its melting point (132°C), ammonia gas slowly evolves; then, several different substances gradually form [37]. By slightly increasing the temperature above the normal melting point of pure urea, the biuret is obtained. When temperature reaches to 600°C, urea decomposes and creates volatile species that can mechanically expand graphite oxide; furthermore, volatile reducing gases which can promote the removal of surface oxygen groups are going to be produced [38]. After synthesis of the RGO, the obtained RGO was modified by using N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-RGO) to produce an efficient absorbent that has a lot of surface functional groups and high surface area.

Graphite, nitric acid (65%), sulphuric acid (98%), ethylene glycol and hydrogen peroxide (30%) were purchased from Merck Company. Potassium permanganate, urea and ethanol were kindly provided by Ghatran Shimi Co (Tehran, Iran). All materials and solvents were used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout. GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) were used for all chemical analyses. The synthesized materials were characterized using various analytical methods. XRD patterns were recorded by a Philips, X-ray diffractometer using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation. The morphology and size of graphene were measured using scanning electron microscope (SEM), LEO instrument model 1455VP. Prior to taking images, the samples were coated with a very thin layer of Pt to make the sample surface conducting and prevent charge accumulation. FT Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained as potassium bromide pellets in the range of 400–4000cm−1 with a Nicolet-Impact 400D spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were taken on a confocal Raman microscope (SENTERRA (2009); BRUKER, Germany) with high-energy laser diodes. The power and the wavelength of the laser are 10mW and 785nm, respectively. The confocal depth resolution and the objective lens used for the Raman measurements are 2μm slit and 50 x, respectively. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of nanopowders were taken by a JEM-2100 with an accelerating voltage of 100kV equipped with a high resolution CCD Camera. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) were carried out on a Mettler TG50 instrument under argon flow at a uniform heating rate of 5°Cmin−1 in the range 25–800°C. The surface area of the product was determined by using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique with Micromeritics (Gemini) in the range of relative pressures from 0.0 to 1.0. Before employing, the sample was degassed at 200°C for 2h.

Graphite oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphite powder using Hummer's method [3] but with some modifications [33]. In a typical reaction, first 2g of graphite fine powder and 2g of sodium nitrate were mixed together followed by the addition of 100ml of concentrated sulphuric acid under constant stirring for 4h. After that, the reaction vessel was placed in ice bath and 10g of potassium permanganate was added gradually to the mixture. Take care that in this step the temperature should be less than 20°C to prevent overheating and explosion. Stirring was continued for 2h in this condition. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was transferred to a pre-heated oil bath and stirred at 35°C for 12h. The obtained mixture was diluted by addition of 200ml of deionized water under vigorous stirring for another 2h.

After that, the mixture was heated to 98°C and stirred for another 12h continuously. The obtained mixture was cooled to room temperature and the suspension was further treated with 30% hydrogen peroxide solution (35ml) to ensure the completion of reaction with KMnO4. After 1h stirring, finally the resulting mixture was filtrated and washed with copiously amount of deionized water to completely remove the residual salts and acids followed by filtration and drying at 60°C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expansion and reduction of GO to RGO
ce:section.ce:para: A 1:1M ratio of GO:urea was mixed together and pulverized in an agate mortar to obtain a uniform distribution of solids. More pulverization time leads to improved contact between the GO and reduction agent. After that, the obtained GO-urea mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25mL) and maintained at 160°C for 48h (optimized conditions) without adding any solvent. As we know, when urea heats up to its melting point (132°C), it decomposes and gas products helps to reduction of GO. The temperature of reaction was chosen 160°C because urea decomposition starts at temperatures in excess of 152°C [39]. More reaction time has important impact on production more reductant gases. Then the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature and the reaction product was taken out with a forceps.

Subsequently, the obtained product was dipped into 50ml deionized water, stirred for 30min and then centrifuged at 7000rpm for 5min to remove any unreacted urea. The reaction product was re-dispersed in ethylene glycol, sonicated for 60min with a power output of ∼300W and a black suspension was formed. After sonication a stable black suspension was obtained, this suspension left to immobilize for a week and then the supernatant was centrifuged at 15000rpm for 10min to gather all RGO product. Finally as obtained RGO washed with acetone twice and dried at 50°C overnight and kept for further characterization. The synthesis procedure has been summarized in shame 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped graphene quantum dots were synthesized according to previous published procedures [40]. 0.63g anhydrous citric acid and was dissolved into 20ml deionized water, and stirred to form a clear solution. After that, 0.54g Urea was added to the above solution and mixed to obtain a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 50ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 170° for 4h in an electric oven. The final product had green color and was collected by adding methanol into the solution and centrifuged at 6000rpm for 5min. The solid can be easily redispersed into water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of RGO modified with N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-RGO)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1g of RGO was dispersed in 40ml deionized water using ultrasound bath (300W) for 15min.

In the second step, 0.05g N-GQDs was added to the mixture and sonicated for another 15min. The mixture was was transferred into a 50ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated to 170° for 4h in an electric oven. The final product was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 5min. Products were washed with copiously amount of deionized water and ethanol and collected by centrifuge. The obtained sediment was dried at 75°C under vacuum for 10h to obtain N-RGO.

The basic dye, methylene blue (MB), Rhodamine B (RhB) and Methyl orange (MO) (S.D. Fine Chemicals, 85% dye content) was used as such without further purification to prepare the simulated waste water. An accurately weighed quantity of dyes (0.02g) was dissolved in double distilled water to prepare the stock solution (100mg/L). The pH of dye solutions were adjusted to 7 by adding diluted HNO3 or NaOH.

The as synthesized GO and RGO are characterized by X-Ray Diffraction Analysis (XRD), Fourier Transform- Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Raman Spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscope (TEM), BET & Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA).

Powder XRD patterns of graphite, graphite oxide and as prepared RGO are shown in Fig. 1 . For both pristine graphite and synthesized RGO a peak related to the periodic lamellar structure of graphite at 2θ=26.44 corresponding to the organized layer structure with an interlayer distance of 0.34nm along the (002) orientation can be seen in diffractograms [41]. It's obviously that, compared with graphite, the peak intensity of the prepared RGO is decreased. This observation is due to the exfoliation of pristine graphite into a few-layer graphene sheets (Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b displays magnified XRD pattern around (002) reflection of materials in the region of 2θ=15 to 40°. As seen, in compared to the pattern of graphite, the diffraction peak of the as prepared RGO is slightly shifted to a lower angles (from 2θ=26.44 to 2θ=26.12degree), indicating an increase in the interplanar distance determined from Bragg's law (2dsinθ=nλ) due to the presence of some oxygen containing functional groups (Fig. 1b) [42]. Also since there is no reflection peak for graphite oxide around (002) orientation, this is a reason for true oxidation of graphite and increasing of the c-axis interlayer spacing to 0.8–1.2nm and completely destruction the 0.34nm graphite inter layer spacing as shown by the red line in Fig. 1b.

Fig. 2 shows the magnified XRD pattern around (001) reflection of GO and RGO in the region of 2θ=10 and 25°. Results indicate that the GO synthesized by modified Hummer's method has peak diffraction at 2θ=12.2° but there is no similar peak for RGO sample. So the elimination of the peak at 26.44° and appearance of the peak at 10°, proves that after the chemical treatment and oxidation, the GO product is completely oxidized, indicating an increase in interlayer distance from 0.34nm to 0.85nm [43].

The infrared absorption bands identify molecular components, chemical functional groups and structures. Here FT-IR characterization was used to investigate the bonding interactions in graphite before and after the oxidation process. Fig. 3 shows FT-IR spectrum for GO and RGO samples. For GO, the broad band at 3440cm −1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of surface hydroxyl (OH) groups and another band around 1243cm−1 is related to the bending vibration of the HOH bonds. These peaks have high intensity due to large amount of hydroxyl functional groups in graphite oxide sample. It assumes the intensities and sharpness of the bands have direct relation with the amount of sample present. Therefore, it can be concluded that because of presence large amount of polar functional groups, the GO sample has strong hydrophilicity property. As displayed in IR spectrum, compared with the pristine GO, the intensity of these bands has been decreased considerably after interaction and reduction by urea and this is an evident for almost complete reduction of GO during the reaction with urea. A weak intensity for hydroxyl groups at the wave number of 3440cm−1 still appears for the rGO sample. We think that the remained hydroxyl groups may relates to water absorbed in KBr during FT-IR analysis. On the other hand such intensity for graphene is normal and it can be seen in previous works [44].

Three peaks at 1711, 1414 and 1108cm−1 can be respectively assigned to the carbonyl, hydroxyl, and epoxyl functional groups on the surface of the GO specimen, confirming the presence of oxygen containing functional groups after the oxidation treatment. The appearance of vibration band in the medium frequency area, at approximately 680cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CO group of carboxylic acid and carbonyl groups present at the edges of GO [42]. Both synthesized GO and RGO samples have absorption peaks at 2925cm−1 and 2850cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching vibrations frequency of CH2 groups. Finally, the absorption band at approximately 1641cm−1 is ascribed to CC bending vibrations. Checking IR spectrum before and after reduction by urea reveals that these three recent bands are stable and still remained before and after the reduction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of GO, graphene sheets and N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM micrographs of graphite, GO and synthesized RGO are given in Fig. 4 . From SEM images it is clear that how the sheets have accumulated together for graphite specimen in Fig. 4a. It is clearly obvious that graphite is expanded after interaction with oxidation agents for GO sample in Fig. 4b. SEM micrographs of urea reduced sample have well defined and it is possible to distinguish the edges of individual exfoliated graphene flakes. This observation reveals that the synthesized RGO has layered structure and most of the GO are more efficiently exfoliated to obtain ultrathin sheets (Fig. 4c). Also, this result confirms the advantage of using the urea as intercalation- reduction agent and the impact of further ultra-sonication.

SEM images related to N-GQDs are depicted in Fig. 5 in 1μ, 500nm and 200nm magnifications. As shown, each carbon sphere is made of many smaller dots with middle size of about 7nm. Due to aggregation of the graphene dots, this size maybe more smaller in case of using TEM for characterization.

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful and versatile tool that can be used to probe and characterization almost every physical feature of graphene and carbon based materials including defects, doping, edge and strain [45–48]. In the Raman spectrum of graphene and carbon based materials two specified peaks are clearly visible, corresponding to the so-called D and G bands. The appeared G and D bands of carbon martials are related to the E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atom and the breathing mode of ƙ-point phonons of A1g symmetry, respectively [49].

Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectra of the natural graphite, GO and as synthesized RGO. As seen, all the Raman spectra display two prominent peaks of the D band and G band. The GO is characterized by typical bands, D band at 1322cm−1 which representatives of sp3 carbon hybridization and G-band peak ataround 1597cm−1 which is characteristic peak of graphitic sheets, corresponding to a well-defined sp2 carbon-type structure [50]. Previously it has been proved that the intensity of the D peak is strongly relates to the disorder degree or presence of defects within the hexagonal graphitic structure of graphene or graphene oxide sheets. Also the G band associate with the first-order scattering of the stretching vibration mode E2g observed for sp2 carbon domains [50].

In the Raman spectrum for GO the intensity of D band is lower than G band clearly. But after the reduction of GO to RGO by urea, the intensity of D-band is partially increased while its position in spectrum is remained unchanged. Also, as seen in Raman spectrum for RGO, reduction process is caused to decrease the intensity of G-band and its place is shifted to lower frequency region (1583cm−1). Generally, the intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is a factor used to estimate and compare the disorder degree of graphene and graphene oxide [51]. The D/G ratio of the reduced GO is higher than that of GO, indicating an increase in the number of smaller graphene domains upon reduction of GO. Increase in the number of monolayer graphene and decrease the sheets size, results to increase in average size of sp3 carbon domains. As displayed in Fig. 6, after reduction by urea, the ID/IG ratio for RGO product (1.33) is higher than related amount for GO sample (0.96). The decreasing in ID/IG ratio suggests a decline in average size of sp2 carbon domains of GO and increase in number of new graphitic domains. All this observations is further evidence confirming the efficient reduction of GO to RGO [52].

The peak at the shoulder of G band can be attributed to remained graphitic component in the sample. Because expanding process causes to decreasing the intensity of the G-band and will shift its place to lower frequency region.

By analyzing a large number of TEM images, paying close attention to the uniformity of the sample and image background, the thickness statistics of generate flakes can be determined [18]. Results of The TEM analysis of RGO product are shown in format of images at low and higher magnifications in Fig. 7 . The graphene sheet shows partially overlapped morphology with a low contrast, that is an indication of few layer flakes (Fig. 7a). The obtained images reveal that proposed approach produce large flakes of graphene that have a few layers thick. The appearance of transparent graphene flake in the TEM analysis and one fold that is appeared as only one dark line proves the presence of single-layer graphene (Fig. 7b). Also presence of transparent and silky sheets of as prepared RGO in TEM image corroborates its stability under high energy electron beam [53].

Thermal properties of the parent graphite, GO and RGO were studied by TGA analysis and the obtained curves are shown in Fig. 8 . All samples display three significant weight loss events, so three main final points of weight loss (200, 500 and 750°C) was considered. TGA plot for parent graphite revealed that this sample relatively is stable to thermal treatment below 800°C under inert atmosphere and just a small mass decrease occurs (2.12% total weight loss). We considered three weight loss stages for GO, but the major weight loss was observed in two steps below 200 (50–150) and 500°C (200−300). The first weight loss corresponds to the fsem, loss of adsorbed and bound water, and the decomposition of thermally unstable oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy functional groups on the carbon surface (−5.3%) (Fig. 8b) [54]. As we know, GO is thermally unstable and it starts to lose mass upon heating even below 100°C, but TGA results reveal that GO loses major weight by 31.1% in between 200 and 500°C with the fastest rate (a total weight loss of 36.4wt% at 500°C), which is attributed to the loss of the labile oxygen functionalities i.e. carboxylic groups on GO [55]. A slower weight loss rate appears from 500°C to 750°C for the GO sample can be attributed to the decomposition and pyrolysis of carbonyl, ether or quinine groups remained on GO sheets [56].

In contrast, compared with the GO, obtained RGO sample exhibited no significant mass loss in the range of 200–300°C and the majority of total mass decline corresponds to the desorption of adsorbed water (Fig. 8c). Between 500 and 750°C, a slower mass loss was happened and can be attributed to CO, CO2, and steam release from the remained oxygen functional groups. Since urea and its solid thermal decomposition byproducts are well soluble in water, washing final product with water caused to no remaining solid residual in obtained RGO. Overall, RGO sample reached a total weight loss of 11.01wt% at 750°C, much lower than 39.3wt% of the GO sample. This result suggests that most of the oxygen functionalities have been removed from the GO during the urea reduction process. All of these observations prove that urea has a significant role as expanding and reluctant agent to synthesize RGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of surface modification on the adsorption efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: As it can be seen in Fig. 9 , after modification the RGO surface by N-GQDs, the specific picks has been emerged in the IR spectrum. Three peaks at 3260, 1696 and 775cm−1 can be respectively assigned to stretching vibration of NH bonds, stretching vibration of CN bonds and bending vibrations of NH2 bonds. This observation indicates successful incorporation of N-GQDs to the RGO substrate. Adsorption experiments were conducted with RGO and N-RGO samples at constant adsorbent dosage (0.05g of adsorbent) and 50ml of the wastewater with 30ppm concentration- pH (neutral), and ambient temperature (26°C) for 25min. Previous works have shown that GO and RGO are capable of interacting with organic dye molecules such as rhodamine B and methylene blue through π–π conjugation and can thus facilitate the adsorption process [57,58]. So we believe that surface modification of graphene by highly functionalized carbonic materials such as N-GQDs can promote the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent.

According to the Lambert's law, the analyte molecular concentration in solution can be linearly related with theabsorbance of the solution,
(1)
A =
K b
C
where Kb is slope of the standard calibration curve (Fig. 10 ). The Kb values for MB, RhB and MO obtained to be 21,540, 17,440 and 20,211L·mo−1 respectively. Now for each of the dyes since the absorbance is measurable, given the Kb, the concentration value of the unknown samples can be derived using Eq. (1). On the other hand, one of the most critical parameters for an adsorbent is saturated adsorption capability (Q), which can be obtained from the following formula:
(2)
Q =
C 0
− Ceq
·
M w
V / m

In this equation Q (mg·g−1) is the saturated molecular amount at equilibrium adsorption, C0 (mol·L−1) is the initial molecular concentration in solution and Ceq (mol·L−1) is molecular concentration at equilibrium adsorption, Mw (mg·mol−1) and V (L) denotes the molecular weight of the adsorbate and the solution volume, and m (g) is the mass loading of the adsorbent in an adsorption process. For obtaining the Q value, typically, 0.05g of the N-RGO adsorbent was dispersed in 2mL DI water and sonicated to obtain a well dispersed suspension. At the next step, an aqueous solution with concentration of 40ppm (8.35×10−5 M for RhB, 1.22×10−4 M for MO and 1.25×10−4 M for MB, 48mL) were respectively prepared. The previous stock suspension was added to each of the dye vessels and all of these mixtures were then kept still for 12h, after which the adsorbents were removed by a centrifuge process and the remaining solutions were analyzed by absorption spectroscopy. A similar experiment was designed to compare the saturated adsorption capability for RGO sample too and the obtained results were depicted in Fig. 11 . After 720min, the absorbance was measured and given the Kb, the Q value of the adsorbent for each dyes derived, which is calculated to be about 37.99mg·g−1 for the as-prepared MO. As can be seen the rate of adsorption is low and it maybe happened because of low rotation speed of the solution. This event leads to decrease the concentration of dye molecules around the adsorbent, therefore kinetic of the adsorption will decrease.

According to the experiment results, for all dye samples, N-RGO has more adsorption capability in comparison with RGO that at similar condition studied. More experiments proved that decolouriztion of wastewater was 100% and 83.1% by N-RGO and RGO samples respectively, at 20mg/L of MO dye concentration after 30min. In this step, the main goal is to show the synergic effect of graphene quantum dots which this observation can approve that. However, as we know in the industry, even 1% increasing in reaction yield is valuable. So this proves that adding just a little N-GQDs to the graphene substrate will improve its performance. The difference in adsorption capacity can be attributed to better physical structure and surface area of N-RGO than RGO. Due to more surface functional groups for N-RGO sample, (like OH, CO and NH groups), N-RGO has more interaction with dye molecules and adsorption capability will enhanced.

Table 2 shows the maximum adsorption capacities of the N-RGO for three dyes follow an order of MO>RhB>MB. The surface area is not exclusive factor to determine the adsorption capability of the adsorbents. Since MB has the dimensions of 1.43nm×0.61nm×0.4nm, this large molecule size makes it difficult for MB to penetrate into the micro-mesopores of N-RGO [59]. A molecular sieving effect will seriously reduce the normalized adsorption capacity of N-RGO because maybe the pore width is narrower than the molecular size of MB or because the shape of the pores does not allow the MB molecules to penetrate into the micro-mesopores. Another reason for the high-normalized adsorption capacity can be attributed to π–π electron donor acceptor interactions with graphene surfaces [60]. RhB has C ̶ C double bonds and contains π electrons which can easily interact with the π electrons of benzene rings on N-RGO through π–π electron coupling comparing to MB, RhB has larger amount of π electrons to interaction between dye molecules and N-RGO, This property makes N-RGO has the higher adsorption capability for RhB in compare with MB.

The specific surface area of the N-GQDs sample and N-RGO composite were evaluated by using the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms as shown in Fig. 12 . Results reveal the stepwise adsorption and desorption branch corresponding to the type of IV isotherm, indicating the presence of mesoporous nanomaterial. The calculated surface area of N-GQDs sample was about55m2 g−1 while the specific surface area of the N-RGO composite was obtained about 165m2/g−1 and the isotherm exhibits type IV pattern again with hysteresis loop, indicating of mesoporous nanostructures. The results show wider surface area for the N-RGO sample confirming reasonable strategy for synthesizing of an adsorbent with higher adsorption efficiency.

In principle, there are two kinds of interactions that could be involved in this system which improves the adsorption capacity, the physisorption on the prepared high surface area and the π–π conjugation with RGO surface. The interaction is not likely to be a simple physisorption either, which can be testified through comparing the adsorption capacities per unit surface area of N-GQDs (surface area: 55m2·g−1) and the N-RGO hybrid (surface area: 165m2·g−1) to the same dye (e.g., MO). According to the adsorption capacity test, results clearly indicating a stronger and more efficient adsorption mechanism other than the simple physisorption. So the π–π conjugation should be the decisive one responsible for the adsorption.

Graphite oxide was synthesized by modified hummer method. The existence of oxygen functional groups was confirmed by XRD and presence of CO and CC groups by FT-IR characterizations. In the following, the expansion of graphite oxide in compare to graphite sheets is confirmed by SEM image. More expansion of sheets and reduction of previous product occurred when GO was pulverize with urea. The mixture was heated in a close vessel at 160°C for 48h and reaction performed under environment atmosphere. These claims were approved by results of SEM, Raman and TEM characterizations.

More time thermal decomposition of urea helps to efficient reduction of GO at partly low temperature inside the reaction vessel. FT-IR and TGA analysis provided strong evidence for the elimination of labile oxygen containing groups from the surface of GO. TEM analysis was proved single layer graphene production. So the experimental results showed that urea is an efficient reducing agent in eliminating oxygen-containing groups from GO and can be introduced as agent with multiple functions in the process, such as reductant and expansion agent. Thus, urea can compete with hydrazine as widely employed, but highly toxic in terms of reducing agent. The as synthesized RGO exhibit unique properties and can be applied in variety of applications. Main benefits of this technique in compare to traditional hydrazine reduction are the low-cost, environmentally friendly approach and simple product isolation process. Surface modification with N-GQDs will significantly improve the adsorption efficiency. Results indicated that N-RGO sample can completely remove MO dye just in 30min. This rote is economically effective because we synthesized N-GQDs via a very cost effective method using commercial and nontoxic materials (citric acid water, and Urea). Besides, N-GQDs with spherical morphology and small nanoscale size have high surface area that can promote the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent. Results indicate that mixing the graphene substrate with a little amount of N-GQDs can promote the absorption capacity for Rhodamine b to 9%. However, as we know in catalytic and industrial reactions even 1% increasing in reaction yield is valuable. So this proves that adding just a little N-GQDs to the graphene substrate will improve its performance.
Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/89290).
